---
title: "Salary & Compensation Trends"
author: "Nutchanon Chaiyaratana and Afreen Alam"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  freeze: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---
## Overview

This section analyzes salary and compensation patterns in 2024 job postings using the Lightcast dataset. The goal is to understand how salaries vary across occupations, industries, locations, employment types, remote work arrangements, and AI-related skills.

Because salary information is not available for every job posting, this analysis focuses on records with valid salary values greater than zero when comparing salary outcomes.

## Load Prepared Data

This section loads the prepared analysis tables created in the data preparation step. The salary analysis page uses these saved Parquet files directly instead of rebuilding relational tables and AI classifications.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| output: false

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when
from IPython.display import display
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

# Load prepared tables created by data_cleaning.qmd
job_postings_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_postings.parquet")
locations_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")
industries_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")
ai_job_ids = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/ai_job_ids.parquet")
jobs_with_ai = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/jobs_with_ai.parquet")

# Register SQL views
job_postings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")
locations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("locations")
industries_df.createOrReplaceTempView("industries")
ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")
jobs_with_ai.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs_with_ai")

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled
```

The table above is used as an exploratory step. It searches broadly for AI-related terms in Lightcast skill names. After reviewing these results, I use the confirmed AI-related skills to classify job postings into AI-related and non-AI groups.

## Analysis 1: Salary Differences Between AI and Non-AI Careers

This analysis classifies job postings as AI-related if they include AI or machine learning signals from either Lightcast skill tags, job titles, or job descriptions. Jobs without these AI-related signals are classified as non-AI. This combined method helps reduce missed AI-related postings that may not have AI skills tagged directly.

```{python}
ai_vs_non_ai_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)), 2) AS avg_salary,
    PERCENTILE_APPROX(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE), 0.5) AS median_salary,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.id) AS job_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.title_clean) AS unique_job_titles
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
ORDER BY
    avg_salary DESC
""")

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd = ai_vs_non_ai_salary.toPandas()

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_share"] = (
    ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"] / ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"].sum()
)

style_table(ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd, {
    "avg_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "median_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_job_titles": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.1%}"
})
```
AI-related postings are identified using both Lightcast skill tags and AI-related keywords in job titles and descriptions. This combined classification reduces the chance of missing AI-related jobs that are not tagged with AI skills directly. The comparison uses only postings with valid salary values.

## How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
```{python}

salary_distribution = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
""")

salary_distribution_pd = salary_distribution.toPandas()

import plotly.express as px

fig_ai_salary = px.violin(
    salary_distribution_pd,
    x="career_type",
    y="salary",
    color="career_type",
    points=False,
    title="Salary Distribution: AI vs Non-AI Careers"
)

fig_ai_salary.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

fig_ai_salary.show()
```
Insight: AI-related roles command higher salaries than non-AI roles, as seen from the elevated Q1, median and Q3 quartiles. 
However, the maximum salary an AI-related role can bag is lower than a non-AI role.

## What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
```{python}
state_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    l.state_name,
    j.career_type,
    AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)) AS avg_salary
FROM jobs_with_ai j
JOIN locations l
    ON j.location_id = l.location_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    l.state_name,
    j.career_type
""")

state_salary_pd = state_salary.toPandas()

pivot_df = (
    state_salary_pd
    .pivot(
        index="state_name",
        columns="career_type",
        values="avg_salary"
    )
    .reset_index()
)
pivot_df = pivot_df.dropna(subset=["AI-related", "Non-AI"])
pivot_df["premium_dollars"] = (
    pivot_df["AI-related"] -
    pivot_df["Non-AI"]
)

pivot_df["premium_pct"] = (
    (pivot_df["AI-related"] -
     pivot_df["Non-AI"])
    / pivot_df["Non-AI"]
) * 100

# Add state abbreviation column for Plotly choropleth map
state_abbrev = {
    "Alabama": "AL", "Alaska": "AK", "Arizona": "AZ", "Arkansas": "AR",
    "California": "CA", "Colorado": "CO", "Connecticut": "CT", "Delaware": "DE",
    "Florida": "FL", "Georgia": "GA", "Hawaii": "HI", "Idaho": "ID",
    "Illinois": "IL", "Indiana": "IN", "Iowa": "IA", "Kansas": "KS",
    "Kentucky": "KY", "Louisiana": "LA", "Maine": "ME", "Maryland": "MD",
    "Massachusetts": "MA", "Michigan": "MI", "Minnesota": "MN", "Mississippi": "MS",
    "Missouri": "MO", "Montana": "MT", "Nebraska": "NE", "Nevada": "NV",
    "New Hampshire": "NH", "New Jersey": "NJ", "New Mexico": "NM", "New York": "NY",
    "North Carolina": "NC", "North Dakota": "ND", "Ohio": "OH", "Oklahoma": "OK",
    "Oregon": "OR", "Pennsylvania": "PA", "Rhode Island": "RI",
    "South Carolina": "SC", "South Dakota": "SD", "Tennessee": "TN", "Texas": "TX",
    "Utah": "UT", "Vermont": "VT", "Virginia": "VA", "Washington": "WA",
    "West Virginia": "WV", "Wisconsin": "WI", "Wyoming": "WY",
    "District of Columbia": "DC"
}

pivot_df["state_code"] = pivot_df["state_name"].map(state_abbrev)
pivot_df = pivot_df.dropna(subset=["state_code"])

fig_ai_premium = px.choropleth(
    pivot_df,
    locations="state_code",
    locationmode="USA-states",
    color="premium_pct",
    scope="usa",
    hover_name="state_name",
    hover_data={
        "AI-related": ":,.0f",
        "Non-AI": ":,.0f",
        "premium_dollars": ":,.0f",
        "premium_pct": ":.1f",
        "state_code": False
    },
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn",
    title="AI Salary Premium (%) by State"
)

fig_ai_premium.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=550
)

#fig_ai_premium.show()

```
Insight: South Dakota and New Mexico have the highest salary premiums for AI-related roles, paying up to 42% more on average for AI roles than non-AI roles.
On the other side, Kentucky penalizes AI-related roles, paying them ~4% lesser on average than non-AI roles.


## Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
```{python}
remote_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') AS remote_type,
    CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings
WHERE
    salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
    AND COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') != 'Unknown'
""")

remote_salary_pd = remote_salary.toPandas()
remote_salary_pd["remote_type"] = remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].astype(str)
remote_salary_pd = remote_salary_pd[
    (~remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].isna()) & 
    (~remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].isin(["None", "Unknown", "nan", "null", ""]))
]
fig_remote = px.box(
    remote_salary_pd,
    x="remote_type",
    y="salary",
    color="remote_type",
    title="Salary by Remote Work Arrangement"
)

fig_remote.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

#fig_remote.show()
```
Insight: Remote jobs pay higher than non-remote and hybrid roles as seen from the highest Q1, median and Q3 values for remote roles. Non-remote and hybrid
roles have very similar salary distributions, with hybrid role salaries showing more spread.


## What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?
```{python}
industry_salary_monthly = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    i.naics_2022_6_name AS industry,
    MONTH(j.posted_date) AS month_num,
    DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM') AS month,
    ROUND(
        AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)),0) AS avg_salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN industries i
    ON j.industry_id = i.industry_id
WHERE
    YEAR(j.posted_date) = 2024
    AND j.salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    i.naics_2022_6_name,
    MONTH(j.posted_date), DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM')
ORDER BY industry, MONTH(j.posted_date)
""")

industry_salary_monthly_pd = industry_salary_monthly.toPandas()

top_industries = (
    industry_salary_monthly_pd
    .groupby("industry")["avg_salary"]
    .mean()
    .nlargest(12)
    .index
)

plot_df = industry_salary_monthly_pd[
    industry_salary_monthly_pd["industry"].isin(top_industries)
]
month_order = [
    "May", "Jun",
    "Jul", "Aug", "Sep"
]

fig_industry = px.line(
    plot_df,
    x="month",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="industry",
    markers=True,
    category_orders={"month": month_order},
    title="Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)"
)

buttons = []
buttons.append(dict(
    method="update",
    label="All Industries",
    args=[{"visible": [True] * len(top_industries)},
      {"title": "Industry Performance Overview"}]
))

# Create buttons to isolate each industry
for i, industry in enumerate(top_industries):
    visibility = [False] * len(top_industries)
    visibility[i] = True
    
    buttons.append(dict(
        method="update",
        label=industry,
        args=[{"visible": visibility},
              {"title": f"Industry Performance: {industry}"}]
    ))

fig_industry.update_layout(
    title={
        "text": "Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)",
        "x": 0.0,        
        "xanchor": "left",
        "y": 0.97
    },
    updatemenus=[
        dict(
            active=0,
            direction="down",
            x=0,          # align with left edge of plot
            y=1.08,       # below title
            xanchor="left",
            yanchor="top",
            buttons=buttons
        )
    ],
    margin=dict(
        t=100,   # increase top margin
        r=120
    ),
    template="plotly_white",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Average Salary"
)

fig_industry.show()
```
Insight: Agents for Wireless Telecommunications Services saw the most volatile salary swings, 
dropping to a low of $100k in June before surging back to lead the pack near $340k by September. Meanwhile, most other industries maintained relatively 
flat and stable wage trends throughout 2024.

## Salary & Compensation Trends Dashboard

A visual summary comparing AI and traditional compensation packages across states, work arrangements, and industries.

::: {#dashboard-layout layout="[[50, 50], [50, 50]]"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-ai-salary
#| fig-cap: "AI vs Non-AI Salaries"

fig_ai_salary.update_layout(
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=40, b=20),
    height=400
)
fig_ai_salary.show()
```
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-ai-premium
#| fig-cap: "AI Premium (%) by State"

fig_ai_premium.update_geos(scope='usa')
fig_ai_premium.update_layout(
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=40, b=20),
    height = 400,
    coloraxis_colorbar=dict(
        len=0.6,           
        thickness=15,      
        yanchor="middle",  
        y=0.5,
        title=dict(font=dict(size=11)),
        tickfont=dict(size=10)
    )

)
fig_ai_premium.show()
```
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-remote
#| fig-cap: "Salary Distribution By Work-Arrangement"

fig_remote.update_layout(
    height=400,                            # Matches right plot height
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=40, b=20)   # Matches right plot padding exactly
)

fig_remote.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-industry
#| fig-cap: "Salary Growth By Industry"

fig_industry.update_layout(
    height=400,
    margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=40, b=20), 
    legend=dict(
        orientation="h", 
        yanchor="top", 
        y=-0.35,              
        xanchor="center", 
        x=0.5,
        font=dict(size=9)     
    )
)
fig_industry.update_xaxes(categoryorder='array', categoryarray=['May', 'Jun', 'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep'])
fig_industry.show()
```
:::

# Key Insights

- The AI Premium: AI-related roles offer a significantly higher median salary and clear premium across almost all U.S. states compared to non-AI positions.
- Work Arrangement Flexibility: Fully remote and hybrid roles match or outpace traditional on-site ("Not Remote") distribution curves, signaling no financial penalty for flexible environments.
- Industry Wage Volatility: Tech-adjacent sectors, particularly Agents for Wireless Telecommunications, exhibit massive monthly wage growth and volatility, while traditional industries like manufacturing remain flat.

# Recommendations for Job Seekers

- Target AI Upskilling: Integrate AI skills such as programming, machine learning, agentic AI capabilities into your current skill set to unlock higher baseline salaries.
- Geographic Arbitrage: Utilize fully remote roles to secure high-paying positions anchored in tech-heavy regions without needing to relocate.
- Timing over Tech-Adjacent Roles: If target industries involve high-volatility salary trends (like telecommunications), expect highly fluid compensation structures and time your negotiations during peak market upswings.
